name: Grant Acedrex
author: Alfonso X, Libro de los Juegos (1283) / reconstruction by J.-L. Cazaux & S. Musser / AGP port
description: Grant Acedrex ("Great Chess"), the large medieval chess variant described in Alfonso X of Castile's Libro de los Juegos (1283), on a 12x12 board with exotic animal pieces. Each side has a King, two Rooks (orthogonal sliders), two Crocodiles (diagonal sliders / modern bishops), two Aancas (a griffon: one diagonal step then an orthogonal slide outward), two Unicorns (a knight-leap then a diagonal slide outward), two Lions ((3,0)+(3,1) leapers) and two Giraffes ((3,2) zebra leapers), plus twelve pawns. On its first move the King may leap two squares (orthogonally or diagonally), jumping over the intermediate square. Pawns step a single square (no double step, no en passant) and promote to the piece that started on the file they reach (the King's-file pawn becomes an Aanca). No castling. The historical dice are omitted -- this is the standard no-dice reconstruction. Checkmate wins; stalemate is a draw.
